entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
Students will read engaging and accessible stories from Roman history that introduce big ideas about what it means to be a citizen. This provides a foundation for further study in Plutarch.

This topic is included in the following course(s): Citizenship: Grade 4

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content: Sensitive students may wait a year to begin Citizen Stories. (The book used in this course contains some violent scenes.)

rows.label: First Citizen Stories
rows.books: Stories From the History of Rome||Historical Atlas of the World 6th Ed.

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

First Citizen Stories
☐ You may want to preread stories so that you are familiar with the pronunciation of the names, and you may also want to preview the content for sensitive students.

☐ As desired, plan for a read-aloud time. Suggestions are provided in the Community Read Aloud Quick Link, but local favorites are highly recommended whenever possible.

books.title: Stories From the History of Rome
Historical Atlas of the World 6th Ed.

blocks.text: Read through the lesson and gather any materials or find any necessary links. If you have not already read the book, you may want to read the selected pages in advance.
Connect back to the previous lesson. Ask students to give a summary of what they remember. (Full narration is not necessary.)
Use a picture, a map, or an engaging question to pique the student's interest in the day's lesson. If necessary, you may talk about one or two unfamiliar words that will be crucial to understanding.
Read the day's passage out loud or have the student read independently.
Have students retell the passage either orally or in writing.
Give the student an opportunity to share thoughts on the lesson and any connections made. You could ask them what they thought about the passage or ask an open-ended question.
Optional: Add to the Citizenship Notebook, Century Chart, and/or Book of Centuries.

lessons.title: 20m First Citizen Stories - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
The Building of Rome
⍞ Materials: Stories from the History of Rome, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ INTRO
Look at the cover of the book. What do you think this book will be about?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.I p.1-4 "There once reigned" - "as a god."

➜ LOCATE
Locate and discuss where the story takes place (Rome, Italy).
⍞ Map Resource: Historical Atlas of the World
"Roman Republic,"  - "City of Rome" p.14-17
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
 Founding of Rome (753 B.C.)

lessons.title: 20m First Citizen Stories - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
The Horatii and the Curiatii
⍞ Materials: Stories from the History of Rome 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.II p.5-8 "In the reign" - "was left alone."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
If you haven't planned for a Community Read Aloud yet, consider adding it to your gathering, meal time, or as a way to lighten the burden of chores! You can find suggestions in Quick Links.

lessons.title: 20m First Citizen Stories - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
The Horatii and the Curiatii (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Stories from the History of Rome 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.II p.8-12 "The Horatius sprang" - "town of Rome."

➜ PICTURE TALK
Look at this picture titled Combat of the Horatii and the Curiatii. It is a mural that was painted by Giuseppe Cesari in 1612. What part of the story is depicted?
∞ Image Link: Combat...Curiatii
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES
 The Horatii & The Curiatii (c.630 B.C.)

★ TEACHER NOTE
Please decide whether you want to read this chapter. This story ends with a Roman man giving his life as a sacrifice for the gods by jumping into a hole in the ground.

lessons.title: 20m First Citizen Stories - Lesson 29

The Caudine Forks
⍞ Materials: Stories from the History of Rome 

➜ STUDY

∞ Map Link: Roman Conquest of Italy

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.XIV p.93-96 "Some distance to" - "food or sleep."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
It's never too late to add a Community Read Aloud! You can find suggestions in Quick Links.

terms.content: · Stories from the History of Rome: Tell the story of Caius Marcius and his mother.
· Stories from the History of Rome: ". . .Romans love justice more than victory." What did this mean in relation to winning the city of Falerii (Ch.9)?
· Stories from the History of Rome: Tell about the fight between the warrior of Gaul and the warrior of Rome.
